title: Anesthesia for Central Nervous System Diseases – MCQ
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav
section: Anesthesia for Central Nervous System Diseases

questions.question: What is the normal intracranial pressure (ICP) in a supine adult?
questions.explanation: Normal ICP in a supine adult ranges from 5–15 mmHg. Values above 20 mmHg are considered raised and require treatment.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Normal ICP

questions.question: The Monro-Kellie doctrine states that the cranial vault contains three components. Which of the following is NOT one of them?
questions.explanation: The Monro-Kellie doctrine states the skull contains brain tissue (~80%), CSF (~10%), and blood (~10%). An increase in any one must be compensated by a decrease in another.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Intracranial Dynamics

questions.question: Which of the following is the most important determinant of cerebral blood flow (CBF)?
questions.explanation: PaCO2 is the most potent physiological regulator of CBF. For every 1 mmHg change in PaCO2, CBF changes by approximately 1–2 mL/100g/min.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Cerebral Blood Flow Regulation

questions.question: Normal cerebral blood flow (CBF) in an adult is approximately:
questions.explanation: Normal CBF is approximately 50 mL/100g/min, with grey matter receiving 80 mL/100g/min and white matter 20 mL/100g/min.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: CBF Values

questions.question: Cerebral autoregulation maintains constant CBF over a MAP range of:
questions.explanation: Cerebral autoregulation operates between MAP of 50–150 mmHg. Below 50 mmHg, ischemia occurs; above 150 mmHg, breakthrough edema may result.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Autoregulation

questions.question: At what PaO2 level does significant cerebral vasodilation begin?
questions.explanation: Significant cerebral vasodilation occurs when PaO2 falls below 60 mmHg. Above this threshold, oxygen has minimal effect on CBF.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Effect of PaO2 on CBF

questions.question: Which intravenous anesthetic agent causes the GREATEST reduction in cerebral metabolic rate (CMRO2)?
questions.explanation: Thiopentone (and other barbiturates) produce the greatest reduction in CMRO2, up to 55–60% reduction. They are used for cerebral protection ('barbiturate coma').
questions.topic: Effects of IV Agents on CBF
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: IV Anesthetics and CMRO2

questions.question: Which intravenous anesthetic agent INCREASES cerebral blood flow and ICP?
questions.explanation: Ketamine increases CMRO2, CBF, and ICP. It is generally avoided in patients with raised intracranial pressure.
questions.topic: Effects of IV Agents on CBF
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Ketamine and ICP

questions.question: Among volatile anesthetic agents, which causes the LEAST increase in ICP?
questions.explanation: Sevoflurane causes the least cerebral vasodilation among volatile agents and has minimal effect on ICP at concentrations <1 MAC. Halothane causes the most increase in ICP.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Inhalational Agents

questions.question: The cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) is calculated as:
questions.explanation: CPP = MAP – ICP. Normal CPP is 70–80 mmHg. CPP below 50 mmHg may lead to cerebral ischemia.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: CPP

questions.question: What is the minimum acceptable CPP to avoid cerebral ischemia?
questions.explanation: CPP should be maintained above 50–60 mmHg to prevent cerebral ischemia. Below 50 mmHg, ischemic changes begin to occur.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: CPP Thresholds

questions.question: The most effective method to rapidly reduce raised ICP during neurosurgery is:
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation (reducing PaCO2 to 30–35 mmHg) causes cerebral vasoconstriction and is the fastest method to reduce ICP. However, its effect is transient (6–12 hours).
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Management of Raised ICP

questions.question: The target PaCO2 during hyperventilation for ICP control is:
questions.explanation: Target PaCO2 of 30–35 mmHg is recommended. Excessive hyperventilation (PaCO2 <25 mmHg) can cause cerebral ischemia due to excessive vasoconstriction.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Hyperventilation Guidelines

questions.question: What is the dose of mannitol used for ICP reduction?
questions.explanation: Mannitol is administered at 0.25–1 g/kg IV over 15–20 minutes. It acts as an osmotic diuretic, drawing water from brain tissue into the intravascular space.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Mannitol

questions.question: Which muscle relaxant is CONTRAINDICATED in patients with raised ICP?
questions.explanation: Succinylcholine can transiently increase ICP due to muscle fasciculations and its effect on cerebral vasculature. However, it may still be used for rapid sequence intubation when the benefit outweighs the risk.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Neuromuscular Blockers

questions.question: The preferred induction agent for neurosurgical patients is:
questions.explanation: Propofol is preferred because it reduces CMRO2, CBF, and ICP while maintaining cerebral autoregulation. It also provides smooth induction and rapid recovery.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Induction Agents

questions.question: During craniotomy in the sitting position, the most feared complication is:
questions.explanation: Venous air embolism (VAE) is the most dangerous complication of sitting position craniotomy. Incidence can be as high as 25–50%. Air enters through open dural venous sinuses.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Complications of Sitting Position

questions.question: The most sensitive monitor for detecting venous air embolism is:
questions.explanation: TEE is the most sensitive monitor for VAE, detecting as little as 0.02 mL/kg of air. Precordial Doppler is the most sensitive non-invasive monitor.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: VAE Monitoring

questions.question: The most sensitive NON-INVASIVE monitor for venous air embolism is:
questions.explanation: Precordial Doppler is the most sensitive non-invasive monitor for VAE, capable of detecting 0.05 mL/kg of air. It produces a characteristic 'mill-wheel' murmur.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: VAE Monitoring

questions.question: In the event of venous air embolism, the IMMEDIATE first step is:

options.A: Place patient in left lateral decubitus position
options.B: Flood the surgical field with saline and cover with wet gauze
options.C: Aspirate air through central venous catheter

questions.explanation: The immediate step is to inform the surgeon to flood the field with saline and apply bone wax to stop further air entry. Other measures follow: discontinue N2O, aspirate air via CVP, and left lateral/Trendelenburg position if hemodynamically unstable.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: VAE Treatment

questions.question: Why should nitrous oxide (N2O) be avoided or discontinued during venous air embolism?

options.B: It expands the air bubble (34 times more soluble than nitrogen)

questions.explanation: N2O is 34 times more soluble in blood than nitrogen. It rapidly diffuses into air bubbles, expanding them and worsening the hemodynamic effects of air embolism.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: N2O in VAE

questions.question: Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score ranges from:
questions.explanation: GCS ranges from 3 (deep coma) to 15 (fully conscious). It assesses Eye opening (1–4), Verbal response (1–5), and Motor response (1–6).
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: GCS

questions.question: A GCS score of 8 or below indicates:
questions.explanation: GCS ≤8 = severe head injury and is an indication for endotracheal intubation to protect the airway. GCS 9–12 = moderate, GCS 13–15 = mild.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: GCS Classification

questions.question: Which IV fluid is preferred in neurosurgical patients?
questions.explanation: Normal saline (0.9% NaCl, 308 mOsm/L) is the preferred fluid because it is slightly hyperosmolar and does not worsen cerebral edema. Glucose-containing fluids are avoided as hyperglycemia worsens ischemic brain injury.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: IV Fluids

questions.question: Why are glucose-containing IV fluids AVOIDED in neurosurgical patients?
questions.explanation: Hyperglycemia promotes anaerobic glycolysis in ischemic brain tissue, leading to lactic acidosis and worsening neuronal injury. Blood glucose should be maintained below 180 mg/dL.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Glucose and Brain Injury

questions.question: The brain receives what percentage of cardiac output?
questions.explanation: The brain receives approximately 15% of cardiac output (about 750 mL/min) despite constituting only 2% of body weight. It consumes approximately 20% of total body oxygen.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Cerebral Blood Supply

questions.question: Cerebral metabolic rate of oxygen (CMRO2) in an adult is approximately:
questions.explanation: Normal CMRO2 is approximately 3.5 mL/100g/min. The ratio of CBF to CMRO2 (flow-metabolism coupling) is normally maintained at approximately 14:1.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: CMRO2

questions.question: Which of the following is the BEST position for a patient with raised ICP?
questions.explanation: Head-up position (15–30°) promotes venous drainage from the brain, reducing ICP. The head should be kept midline to avoid jugular venous compression.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Positioning

questions.question: Cushing's triad (sign of severely raised ICP) consists of:
questions.explanation: Cushing's triad consists of systemic hypertension, reflex bradycardia, and irregular (often slow) respiration. It is a late and ominous sign of brainstem compression.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Cushing's Response

questions.question: Which volatile agent is most commonly used for maintenance of anesthesia in neurosurgery?
questions.explanation: Sevoflurane is preferred because it has minimal effect on ICP at <1 MAC, preserves cerebral autoregulation better than other agents, and allows smooth induction and rapid emergence for neurological assessment.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Inhalational Agent Selection

questions.question: In a patient with supratentorial tumor, the MOST common presenting symptom is:
questions.explanation: Headache (often morning headache that improves on standing) is the most common presenting symptom of supratentorial tumors, caused by raised ICP. Seizures are the second most common.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Clinical Features

questions.question: Posterior fossa tumors are most commonly associated with which sign?
questions.explanation: Posterior fossa tumors commonly present with cerebellar signs (ataxia, nystagmus, dysmetria) and raised ICP due to obstructive hydrocephalus from compression of the 4th ventricle.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Posterior Fossa Surgery

questions.question: During posterior fossa surgery, stimulation of which cranial nerve can cause sudden bradycardia?
questions.explanation: Surgical stimulation near the vagus nerve (CN X) or its nuclei in the floor of the 4th ventricle can cause sudden profound bradycardia or even asystole. Atropine should be readily available.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Cranial Nerve Stimulation

questions.question: Hypertonic saline used for ICP reduction is available in which concentration?
questions.explanation: Hypertonic saline is available in various concentrations (1.8%, 3%, 7.5%, 23.4%). It is an alternative to mannitol for osmotic therapy in ICP reduction, especially in hypovolemic patients.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Hypertonic Saline

questions.question: The advantage of hypertonic saline over mannitol for ICP reduction is:

options.A: It does not cause rebound increase in ICP
options.C: It does not cause electrolyte imbalance

questions.explanation: Unlike mannitol, hypertonic saline does not cause osmotic diuresis (so no hypovolemia) and has less rebound ICP elevation. It also augments intravascular volume, which is beneficial in trauma patients.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Hypertonic Saline vs Mannitol

questions.question: Diabetes insipidus (DI) after pituitary surgery is characterized by:

options.A: Low urine output, high urine specific gravity
options.B: High urine output, low urine specific gravity (<1.005)
options.C: Low urine output, low urine specific gravity
options.D: Normal urine output, high serum sodium

questions.explanation: Central DI presents with polyuria (urine output >250 mL/hr), dilute urine (specific gravity <1.005, osmolality <200 mOsm/kg), and rising serum sodium (hypernatremia). It results from ADH deficiency.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Diabetes Insipidus

questions.question: The treatment of central diabetes insipidus after pituitary surgery is:
questions.explanation: Desmopressin (DDAVP), a synthetic ADH analog, is the treatment of choice for central DI. It can be given intranasally (10–40 mcg), IV, or subcutaneously (1–4 mcg).
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: DI Treatment

questions.question: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) is most commonly graded using which scale?
questions.explanation: The Hunt and Hess scale (Grades I–V) is the most commonly used clinical grading system for SAH severity, correlating with surgical risk and prognosis.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: SAH Grading

questions.question: The most serious complication of aneurysmal SAH that occurs between days 4–14 is:
questions.explanation: Cerebral vasospasm is the most serious delayed complication of SAH, peaking between days 4–14. It can lead to delayed ischemic neurological deficit (DIND) and infarction.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Vasospasm

questions.question: Triple-H therapy for cerebral vasospasm after SAH includes all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Triple-H therapy = Hypertension + Hypervolemia + Hemodilution. It aims to increase CBF through the vasospastic vessels. Hypothermia is not part of triple-H therapy.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Triple-H Therapy

questions.question: Nimodipine is given after SAH because it:

options.B: Prevents cerebral vasospasm and improves neurological outcome

questions.explanation: Nimodipine (a calcium channel blocker with cerebral selectivity) is given for 21 days after SAH. It has been shown to reduce the incidence of ischemic deficits and improve outcomes, though it may not prevent angiographic vasospasm.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Nimodipine

questions.question: During aneurysm clipping, temporary clipping of the parent vessel is used for. What neuroprotective agent may be administered?
questions.explanation: Thiopentone is administered to achieve burst suppression on EEG during temporary clipping, providing cerebral protection by reducing CMRO2 during the period of focal ischemia.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Cerebral Protection

questions.question: In traumatic brain injury (TBI), secondary brain injury is caused by all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: The initial mechanical impact causes PRIMARY brain injury. Secondary brain injury is caused by systemic insults like hypotension (most detrimental), hypoxia, hypercarbia, hyperglycemia, hyperthermia, and seizures.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Primary vs Secondary Brain Injury

questions.question: The SINGLE most detrimental systemic insult in traumatic brain injury is:
questions.explanation: Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) is the single most detrimental secondary insult in TBI, doubling mortality. Even a single episode of hypotension significantly worsens outcome.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Secondary Insults in TBI

questions.question: Awake craniotomy is indicated for surgery near:
questions.explanation: Awake craniotomy allows intraoperative cortical mapping of eloquent areas (speech, motor, sensory cortex) to maximize tumor resection while preserving neurological function.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Awake Craniotomy Indications

questions.question: Lumbar CSF drain placement is contraindicated in:
questions.explanation: Lumbar CSF drainage is contraindicated when there is supratentorial mass with midline shift, as sudden CSF removal from below can cause transtentorial (uncal) herniation.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: CSF Drainage Contraindications

questions.question: Propofol infusion syndrome is characterized by all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Propofol infusion syndrome (with prolonged high-dose infusion >48h, >4 mg/kg/hr) causes severe metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, renal failure, cardiac failure, and lipemia. Hypernatremia is not a feature.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Propofol Infusion Syndrome

questions.question: Emergence from anesthesia in neurosurgery should ideally be:

options.B: Smooth, avoiding coughing and straining (to prevent ICP spikes)
options.C: Delayed by 24 hours in all cases
options.D: Preceded by reversal of all neuromuscular blockade with neostigmine

questions.explanation: Smooth emergence is critical in neurosurgery to avoid coughing, straining, and hypertension, all of which can raise ICP and cause postoperative bleeding. IV lidocaine (1.5 mg/kg) before extubation can help.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Recovery and Extubation

questions.question: The cerebral steal phenomenon occurs when:

options.A: A vasodilator diverts blood away from ischemic areas to normal areas
options.B: A vasoconstrictor shunts blood to ischemic areas
options.C: Hyperventilation improves perfusion to all brain regions equally
options.D: Mannitol selectively improves flow to ischemic brain

questions.explanation: Cerebral steal occurs when vasodilators (like volatile agents) dilate vessels in normal brain, diverting blood AWAY from ischemic regions (which have maximally dilated vessels that cannot dilate further). The inverse (Robin Hood) phenomenon redirects blood TO ischemic areas.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Steal Phenomenon

questions.question: Intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring during neurosurgery includes all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Common neuromonitoring modalities include SSEP (sensory pathway), MEP (motor pathway), EEG (cortical activity), BAEP (brainstem auditory), and direct nerve stimulation. Diaphragm EMG is not a standard neurological monitor.
questions.reference: Anesthesia for CNS Diseases, Section: Intraoperative Monitoring
